Data Exploration - US Equities¶

In this notebook, we explore our data fetching pipline and conduct some exploratory statistical analysis on a small set of US stocks.

  • This notebook will also serve as an initial validation of the Alpaca client API for data, the libraries and virtual environment, as well as our analytics.py file.
  • The methods and concepts in this file are customizable for
    1. tickers
    2. time period
    3. data frequency
  • The volatility clustering analysis is delayed to the later phases of the project.

Imports¶

In [1]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from datetime import datetime
from qre.data.historical import HistoricalDataStore
from qre.analytics.metrics import summary

%matplotlib inline

Loading data¶

In [2]:
tickers = [
    'NVDA',   # Nvidia
    'AAPL',   # Apple
    'MSFT',   # Microsoft
    'GOOGL',  # Alphabet (Google)
    'AMZN',   # Amazon
    'META',   # Meta (Facebook)
    'JPM',    # JPMorgan Chase
    'GS',     # Goldman Sachs
    'V',      # Visa
    'XOM',    # ExxonMobil
    'JNJ',    # Johnson & Johnson
    'WMT',    # Walmart
    'PG',     # Procter & Gamble
    'UNH',    # UnitedHealth
]

start = datetime(2024, 1, 1)
today = datetime.today()
end = datetime(today.year, today.month, today.day)

store = HistoricalDataStore()
closes = pd.DataFrame()

for ticker in tickers:
    df = store.fetch_and_store(ticker, "1d", start, end)
    closes[ticker] = df['close']

Normalized price chart¶

In [4]:
normalized = (closes / closes.iloc[0] * 100 )
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 7))
normalized.plot(ax=plt.gca())
plt.title('Normalized Closing Prices (Base = 100)')
plt.legend(loc='upper left', fontsize='8')
plt.xlabel('Date')
plt.ylabel('Indexed to 100')
plt.grid()
plt.tight_layout()
  • Fixed Alpaca Client API calls for adjustment = Adjustment.ALL to get adj. prices accounting for stock splits and dividends on ex-days. NVDA and WMT had 1-10 stock splits in early 2024.
  • Raw prices are important for execution but not for wealth or signal generation (maybe: price psychology?)
  • NVDA outperfroms all the other stocks in the basket as expected.

Basic Statistics on returns¶

In [5]:
returns = closes.pct_change().dropna()
returns.describe()

In [6]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
returns["AAPL"].hist(bins=50, alpha=0.7, label='AAPL actual', ax=ax, density=True)
x = np.linspace(returns["AAPL"].min(), returns["AAPL"].max(), 100)
from scipy.stats import norm
ax.plot(x, norm.pdf(x, returns["AAPL"].mean(), returns["AAPL"].std()), 'r', label='Normal Distribution')

ax.set_title("AAPL Daily Return Distribution")
ax.set_xlabel("Daily Return")
ax.set_ylabel("Density")
ax.legend()
Out[6]:
<matplotlib.legend.Legend at 0x2d9f8a5c7d0>
In [7]:
print(returns["AAPL"].kurtosis())
print(returns["AAPL"].skew())

12.562422619589176
0.8757767923509413

leptokurtosis (fat tails + sharp peak). This is one of the most well-known properties of financial returns.

A normal distribution spreads its probability more evenly. Real returns concentrate in two places:

  1. Right at the mean — most days are boring, very small moves
  2. Far in the tails — when big moves happen, they're bigger than normal predicts

The region in between (±1 std) is underpopulated compared to normal — fewer "medium-sized" moves than expected.

This is why:

  • VaR (Value at Risk) models that assume normality underestimate tail risk — 2008 was a "25-sigma event" under normal assumptions (essentially impossible), but it happened
  • Kurtosis measures this. Normal distribution has kurtosis = 3. Financial returns typically have kurtosis of 5-10+.

This returns excess kurtosis (pandas subtracts 3), so anything > 0 means fatter tails than normal.

Correlation Analysis (Heatmap)¶

In [8]:
corr = returns.corr()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 8))
im = ax.imshow(corr, cmap='RdBu_r', vmin=-1, vmax=1)

ax.set_xticks(range(len(tickers)))
ax.set_yticks(range(len(tickers)))
ax.set_xticklabels(tickers, rotation=45, ha='right')
ax.set_yticklabels(tickers) 

# Annotate each cell with the value
for i in range(len(tickers)):
    for j in range(len(tickers)):
        ax.text(j, i, f"{corr.iloc[i, j]:.2f}", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=7)

plt.colorbar(im)
plt.title("Daily Return Correlations")
plt.tight_layout()

Note:

  • Tech stocks factor correlation (~0.4)
  • GS and JPM correlation: 0.78 (highest in the basket, investment banks exposed to macro factors like interest rates, deal flow, credit markets etc.), and ~0.4 corr with V (another fin services company)
  • Slight correlation between PG, WMT, JNJ
  • UNH (healthcare stock) no correlation with anything - diversifier?
  • ExxonMobil only slight correlation with financial services (JPM, GS, V)
  • Most stocks have positive corr reflecting the market factor - long-only portfolios cannot diversify systematic risk

Corr ranking (all >= 0.5):

  • GS/JPM
  • META/AMZN
  • AMZN/MSFT
  • AMZN/GOOGL
  • MSFT/NVDA

Rolling Volatility¶

In [9]:
rolling_vol = returns.rolling(window=21).std() * np.sqrt(252)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 7))
for ticker in ["AAPL", "NVDA", "JPM", "UNH"]:        
    ax.plot(rolling_vol[ticker], label=ticker)

ax.set_title("21-Day Rolling Annualized Volatility")
ax.set_ylabel("Annualized Volatility")
ax.set_xlabel("Date")
ax.legend()
ax.grid()
plt.tight_layout()

This pattern is called volatility clustering: big moves follow big moves, small moves follow small moves. It was first observed by Mandelbrot in the 1960s.

Why it happens: When a shock hits (earnings surprise, Fed announcement, geopolitical event), it creates uncertainty. Traders reprice risk, hedging activity increases, more stop-losses trigger - this feeds on itself for a while until the market digests the information and calms down.

Why the "noisy plateaus" look: The 21-day rolling window acts like a memory. One big day enters the window and stays in the calculation for 21 days. When that big day finally drops out of the window, volatility falls abruptly. That's why you see the sharp edges.

For quant trading:

  • Position sizing should adapt - take smaller positions during high-vol regimes
  • A strategy that targets constant portfolio volatility (vol-targeting) needs to estimate this in real-time
  • The non-constant nature of volatility is why the simple sqrt(252) annualization is an approximation - it assumes vol is steady, which it clearly isn't.
In [10]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(14, 10), sharex=True)

for ax, window in zip(axes, [5, 21, 63]):
    vol = returns["NVDA"].rolling(window=window).std() * np.sqrt(252)
    ax.plot(vol)
    ax.set_title(f"NVDA Rolling Vol ({window}-day window)")
    ax.grid()

plt.tight_layout()

Trying to separate the rolling-window length parameter artefact on the charts from measuring actual vol-clustering; an actual measure would the autocorrelation of the squared returns - we will return to this in later phases for GARCH models.

  • For a 21 day rolling window for vol, say there is a large spike in vol caused by a big move in the stock - this will raise the rolling window vol calculation for 21 days until this window excludes that particular day - the plateau could be an artefact of this parameter. However, the actual visual evidence of vol clustering would be if the plateau itself is noisy and tends to go up, meaning newer days raise the calculation more, or when that spike day is out of the calculation, then the vol DOES NOT drop back to its original level, but stays aove the orignal level.
In [11]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 5))
ax.plot(returns["NVDA"] ** 2, color="steelblue", linewidth=0.5)
ax.set_title("NVDA Squared Daily Returns (Proxy for Daily Variance)")
ax.set_ylabel("Return²")
ax.grid()
plt.tight_layout()

My analytics summary table¶

In [12]:
results = {}

for ticker in tickers:
    results[ticker] = summary(returns[ticker])

metrics_table = pd.DataFrame(results).T.round(4)
metrics_table
Out[12]:
annualized_return annualized_volatility sharpe_ratio sortino_ratio max_drawdown calmar_ratio win_rate profit_factor

AAPL 0.2216 0.2749 0.8644 0.8912 -0.3336 0.6641 0.5399 1.1771
MSFT 0.0571 0.2404 0.3513 0.3383 -0.3391 0.1684 0.5263 1.0661
GOOGL 0.5827 0.3032 1.6660 1.7422 -0.2981 1.9551 0.5586 1.3397
AMZN 0.2916 0.3134 0.9729 0.9937 -0.3088 0.9445 0.5348 1.1851
META 0.2780 0.3790 0.8337 0.9028 -0.3415 0.8141 0.5246 1.1728
JPM 0.3021 0.2426 1.2096 1.1517 -0.2442 1.2368 0.5620 1.2489
GS 0.4885 0.2930 1.5042 1.5248 -0.3090 1.5812 0.5586 1.3141
V 0.1010 0.2091 0.5647 0.5370 -0.2038 0.4958 0.5501 1.1104
XOM 0.1976 0.2279 0.9057 0.8598 -0.1892 1.0446 0.5348 1.1626
JNJ 0.1822 0.1734 1.0524 1.0647 -0.1445 1.2614 0.5348 1.2030
WMT 0.4849 0.2223 1.8898 2.0448 -0.2192 2.2119 0.5518 1.4086
PG 0.0214 0.1772 0.2082 0.1958 -0.2115 0.1012 0.5280 1.0361
UNH -0.1214 0.4008 -0.1129 -0.0981 -0.6139 -0.1978 0.5178 0.9766

Winners: NVDA (90% annualized return), GOOGL (58%), WMT (48%), GS (49%). WMT stands out — highest Sharpe (1.89) with relatively low volatility (22%) — the most efficient risk-adjusted performer.

Loser: UNH — the only negative return, worst drawdown (-61%), profit factor below 1.

Patterns:

  • Win rates are all around 52-56% — even the best stocks only go up slightly more than half the days. The money is made by the size of wins vs losses (profit factor), not frequency.
  • Low-vol stocks (JNJ, PG, V) have smaller drawdowns but also lower returns — the classic risk-return tradeoff.
